exec.title: Goldman Sachs India GCC Gets Dual Leadership as Samtani Retires After 9 Years
exec.body: Gunjan Samtani, co-chairman of Goldman Sachs India and head of its Global Capability Centre for nine years, will retire at end-2026. He will be succeeded by two co-heads — Ken Castelino and Balaji Sivasubramanian — who will jointly oversee the 9,000-person Bengaluru and Hyderabad GCC. Roughly half of the workforce are engineers, representing approximately one-third of the bank's global engineering talent.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: Dual succession at a 9,000-person BFSI GCC signals growing leadership complexity as India operations deepen — a structural shift that GCC leaders across sectors should anticipate as founding executives retire and governance models must scale with mandate breadth.

exec.title: NASSCOM-Zinnov 2026: India's GCC Story Shifts from Scale to Ownership
exec.body: At the NASSCOM GCC Summit 2026, the NASSCOM-Zinnov India GCC Landscape report confirmed India hosts 2,117 GCCs employing 2.36 million professionals and generating USD 98.4 billion in market revenue — 32% growth in centre count since FY2021. Ninety-six percent of GCCs established after FY2021 launched with product or portfolio ownership responsibilities from day one, bypassing the traditional crawl-walk-run model entirely.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: The shift from scale-led delivery to ownership-led innovation means GCC leaders must now demonstrate IP accountability and global mandate authority — not just headcount and cost metrics — to sustain headquarters confidence and secure future investment.

exec.title: India's GCCs Evolve from Delivery Arms to Independent AI Buying Centers
exec.body: CRN Asia reports that India's GCCs are no longer execution engines for global headquarters — they are becoming independent budget owners for AI work, with mandates spanning R&D, engineering, and AI centres of excellence. The structural shift is forcing the entire services and partner ecosystem to rewrite account strategy, coverage models, and go-to-market priorities to serve India-based decision-makers directly.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders who secure direct AI budget authority will accelerate capability differentiation; centres still dependent on HQ approval cycles for AI investments will fall progressively behind peers as local AI buying patterns shorten innovation cycles inside India.

exec.title: ISG Provider Lens 2026: GCCs Are Now AI Innovation Hubs, Not Cost Centres
exec.body: The ISG Provider Lens 2026 Global Capability Center Services report confirms that GCCs have moved well beyond cost arbitrage origins, with enterprises building them as hubs for innovation, AI-led operations, and long-term competitive advantage. ISG assessed leading service models including modular GCC-in-a-Box deployments that deliver outcomes such as 50%-plus efficiency gains, elimination of over 30,000 hours of effort generating USD 10 million-plus in savings, and release cycles under 20 minutes.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: Industry analyst validation of AI-first GCC operating models gives leaders a robust business case for accelerating capability investment; GCCs that continue optimising solely on cost risk being de-prioritised in global resource allocation as innovation mandates migrate elsewhere.

themes.title: 83% of India's GCCs Investing in GenAI; 58% Now Scaling Agentic AI Deployments
themes.body: Storyboard18's analysis of India's enterprise and media GCCs finds that 83% are investing in generative AI and 58% are investing in agentic AI — signalling a decisive shift from experimentation to production deployment. GenAI applications are concentrated in customer service (65%), finance (53%), operations (49%), and IT and cybersecurity (45%), with 71% of GCCs growing internal reskilling programmes to sustain deployment velocity.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders must transition from pilot-governance frameworks to production-grade AI operating models immediately; the majority of peers are scaling deployments, and organisations remaining in proof-of-concept mode are compounding capability gaps that will be difficult to close by 2027.

themes.title: EY: India's GCCs Leading the Shift to Intelligent, AI-Native Enterprise Operations
themes.body: EY India's GCC intelligence report identifies that Global Capability Centers are evolving from efficiency-led delivery hubs into agentic AI-first intelligence centres reshaping enterprise operating models. Hybrid pods of humans and AI agents now collaborate on structured delivery workflows, combining precision and scale to expand enterprise capacity without proportional headcount growth — a structural redesign rather than a tooling add-on.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders who redesign team workflows around hybrid AI-human collaboration will unlock non-linear productivity gains; those who layer AI tools onto existing headcount structures will see limited measurable ROI and struggle to justify expanded mandates with global headquarters.

themes.title: EY AIdea India 2026: 47% Enterprises Have Multiple Live AI Use Cases; 24% Deploy Agentic AI
themes.body: EY's AIdea of India 2026 report reveals that 47% of Indian enterprises are running multiple GenAI use cases in production and 24% are deploying agentic AI, marking a decisive shift from experimentation to enterprise-scale execution. Nine in ten leaders cite deployment speed as the primary buy-versus-build AI decision factor, and nearly half of all proofs-of-concept have progressed to production status.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: With nearly half of enterprises in production and 91% prioritising deployment velocity, GCC leaders without a structured AI execution pipeline risk losing innovation mandate to faster-moving enterprise peers — the competitive window for first-mover AI advantage is narrowing rapidly.

themes.title: Agentic AI GCCs Reshape Enterprise Operating Models with Autonomous Hybrid Teams
themes.body: EY India's dedicated agentic AI GCC analysis examines how autonomous AI systems — which plan, act, and adapt independently — are restructuring enterprise delivery across finance, operations, and customer service. GCCs deploying integrated platforms combining BPM, RPA, intelligent document processing, and agentic AI are scaling capacity 2–3x without proportional headcount growth, with reskilling initiatives now standard at 71% of GCCs.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders need an agentic AI readiness roadmap addressing workforce redesign, governance structures, and risk controls — not just technology procurement — to capture value from the agentic transition sustainably and at enterprise scale.

themes.title: GCC TalentScope 2026: Talent Acquisition Shifts to AI-First, Skill-Led Model
themes.body: The GCC TalentScope 2026 India Report from People Matters documents a fundamental shift in GCC talent acquisition: organisations are replacing volume-based hiring with skill-first assessments prioritising AI fluency, domain depth, and cross-functional adaptability. BFSI GCCs focus on specialised roles over bulk recruitment while software product GCCs adopt strategic, innovation-led hiring — both sectors measuring GCC success on mandate complexity rather than headcount size.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC talent leaders who retool hiring infrastructure — from job description design through evaluation frameworks — around AI-native competencies will build faster, higher-quality teams and reduce costly churn driven by misaligned skills and role expectations.

deals.title: Canada's CIBC to Launch Hyderabad GCC with 2,000 Jobs by July 2026
deals.body: Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CIBC) is establishing its first India Global Capability Centre in Hyderabad with a July 2026 launch target, creating 2,000 jobs across banking technology, data analytics, AI, cloud, and cybersecurity. The GCC is being built through ANSR with Talent500 managing hiring, and the commitment was formalised at the Telangana Rising Global Summit 2025.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: CIBC's Hyderabad entry reinforces the city's emergence as India's fastest-growing BFSI GCC destination; competing BFSI GCC leaders should monitor talent pipeline pressure as a new large-scale employer enters the same candidate pool for banking technology and AI engineering talent.

deals.title: Nestlé Selects Hyderabad for New Global Capability Centre
deals.body: Swiss consumer goods giant Nestlé has announced Hyderabad as its India Global Capability Centre location, expected to become operational in coming months. The centre will strengthen Nestlé's digital transformation, supply chain analytics, and global business services capabilities, further diversifying Hyderabad's GCC ecosystem beyond its established BFSI and technology base.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: Nestlé's GCC entry signals that FMCG majors are systematically building India capability centres alongside existing BFSI and tech sectors; this diversification broadens competition for digital and analytics professionals across a wider set of industries.

deals.title: Confluent Launches Dedicated GCC Strategy in India as AI Data Adoption Accelerates
deals.body: Confluent has formalised a dedicated GCC strategy for India, positioning its data streaming platform at the core of AI-driven enterprise workloads as GCCs accelerate real-time data infrastructure investments. The strategy targets India-based GCC decision-makers as independent AI budget owners, aligning with the growing India-first buying centre dynamic where GCC leaders procure directly rather than through global headquarters.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: Purpose-built vendor strategies for India GCC buyers signal market maturation and give GCC leaders stronger negotiating leverage and India-optimised commercial terms; technology leaders should prioritise vendors with dedicated India GCC programmes when evaluating platform investments.

risks.title: GCC AI Talent Crunch: Skill Gaps Widen to 43%; GenAI Engineers Command 30–60% Premium
risks.body: BW Businessworld reports that GCCs face a severe AI talent crunch with skill gaps widening to 43% in AI and data analytics roles. India needs at least 1 million skilled AI professionals by end-2026 but faces a 53% deficit. GenAI engineers now command 30–60% pay premiums over adjacent engineering talent, with roughly 70% of qualified senior GenAI engineers not actively seeking new roles — effectively off the open market.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders must shift from external recruitment to internal reskilling pipelines for GenAI competencies; the external talent pool is effectively captured by existing employers, making retention-focused incentive redesign for current AI practitioners a board-level priority.

risks.title: India DPDP Compliance: Critical 2026–27 Enforcement Deadlines Approach for GCCs
risks.body: India Briefing's compliance analysis documents that all GCCs processing personal data of individuals in India must comply with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, with phased enforcement milestones in November 2026 and May 2027. Significant Data Fiduciaries face penalties up to INR 2.5 billion for violations, and MeitY has proposed accelerating key SDF obligations including DPO appointment and Data Protection Impact Assessments to the November 2026 deadline.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC legal and compliance leaders must treat DPDP as an operational risk with hard financial exposure — not a legal backlog — and immediately assess SDF classification status, accelerate DPO hiring, and commission data mapping and consent architecture work before the November 2026 SDF deadline.

risks.title: GCC India Attrition Persists at 12–15%; AI and Cloud Wage Inflation Reaches 40–50% Annually
risks.body: PlugScale's 2026 GCC attrition analysis shows annual rates hovering at 12–15% across GCCs of varying sizes, creating persistent backfilling costs. More critically, demand for AI and machine learning skills is driving wage inflation with some tech roles seeing 40–50% annual salary rises, threatening to erode India's traditional cost advantage and compress operational margins in AI-intensive functions.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: The confluence of baseline attrition and AI-role wage inflation requires GCC leaders to build differentiated retention strategies beyond compensation — including global exposure, technical ownership, and accelerated career architecture — particularly for mid-to-senior AI practitioners most at risk of competitor poaching.

risks.title: Tier-2 Cities Show 21% GCC Hiring Growth YoY with 10–35% Cost Advantage Over Tier-1
risks.body: Aeries Technology's analysis identifies that emerging GCC cities — Coimbatore, Kochi, Visakhapatnam, Indore, and Ahmedabad — are delivering 21% year-on-year hiring growth with 10–35% lower operational costs versus saturated Tier-1 markets. Cities like Coimbatore are emerging as genuine AI and cybersecurity talent hubs attracting specialist roles that Bengaluru can no longer accommodate affordably, making the hub-and-spoke model increasingly viable.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders with geographic flexibility should accelerate Tier-2 spokes for AI operations, data analytics, and cybersecurity functions before competition intensifies — early movers can lock in talent and real estate advantages before Tier-2 cost premiums converge with Tier-1 markets.

risks.title: India GCC Hiring Boom: NASSCOM Projects 450,000 New GCC Jobs in 2026
risks.body: NASSCOM projects India will add 450,000 new GCC jobs in 2026, with skill-first hiring replacing legacy volume approaches across 1,700-plus active centres in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, Gurgaon, Chennai, and Mumbai. The shift creates strategic windows for GCC leaders to attract high-quality lateral talent from competitors whose volume-hiring strategies have left structural skill mismatches, particularly in AI/ML and platform engineering.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs that have already built skill-first hiring infrastructure are positioned to capture high-quality talent being overlooked by peers still transitioning from volume models — a critical window before the entire market converges on skills-based assessment and narrows the competitive advantage.

risks.title: India's GCC Moment: Strategic AI Ownership Window Opens as Mandate Depth Matures
risks.body: NASSCOM's voices platform identifies that India's GCC strategic moment is real but talent capability will determine whether it lasts: global companies are assigning greater responsibilities — product ownership, AI-led transformation, and global leadership mandates — to India centres. The window to build deep AI capabilities before global competition intensifies for India's talent pool is narrowing, with 506 Forbes Global 2000 companies already operating India GCCs.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders who proactively lobby for expanded AI mandates and demonstrate delivered value in 2026 will secure the resource commitments and headcount authorisations that define competitive positioning through 2030; waiting for HQ to grant ownership will leave strategic ground to faster-moving peers.

competitor.title: ServiceNow Names Kulmeet Bawa India MD and GVP, Signals Enterprise GCC Platform Push
competitor.body: ServiceNow appointed Kulmeet Bawa — formerly of SAP India where he scaled cloud and AI revenue strategy — as Managing Director and Group VP for India and SAARC. The appointment signals ServiceNow's intent to deepen its GCC enterprise platform play, backed by a USD 150 million Ecosystem Ventures fund that launched 1Hub in November 2025 — an AI-powered GCC command-and-control platform purpose-built for the USD 50 billion-plus GCC market.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: ServiceNow's senior India hire and GCC-native platform investment signal that enterprise software vendors are competing aggressively for GCC platform mind-share; GCC leaders evaluating ITSM and workflow automation tools should now factor India-first GCC platform capabilities alongside standard enterprise pricing comparisons.

competitor.title: NVIDIA India: Infosys, Wipro, TCS, Persistent Build Enterprise AI Agent Platforms at Scale
competitor.body: NVIDIA reports that India's top global systems integrators — Infosys, Wipro, TCS, and Persistent — are leading enterprise AI agent deployment using NVIDIA AI Enterprise, transforming back-office and customer service operations. The four firms are collectively upskilling nearly 500,000 developers and consultants to design and deploy AI agents, with Wipro's WEGA-powered solution already deployed for a major US healthcare insurer — demonstrating production viability of agentic systems in GCC-led delivery.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: As India's top SI partners build scaled AI agent practices, GCC leaders gain access to deepening local implementation expertise — but must move quickly to secure priority partner engagement before these capabilities are fully committed to competitor programmes.

competitor.title: LinkedIn Top Companies 2026 India: Infosys, Accenture, Amazon, JPMorgan Lead GCC Employer Ranks
competitor.body: LinkedIn's Top Companies 2026 India ranking places Infosys at the top, with Accenture, Amazon, JPMorgan, and Deloitte among the top ten — all major GCC operators or GCC service providers. The rankings reflect employer brand strength, talent pipeline depth, and career development reputation, highlighting which organisations are winning India's competitive talent acquisition race and attracting the highest-quality candidates.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders whose organisations are not visible in employer brand rankings face a compounding disadvantage in talent acquisition; proactive employer brand investment — including LinkedIn-native content and employee advocacy programmes — is a measurable competitive differentiator in a market where top talent chooses employers, not the reverse.

talent.title: NASSCOM: New GCC Talent Playbook Shifts from Volume Hiring to Value-Led Strategy
talent.body: NASSCOM's latest GCC talent analysis documents the industry-wide shift from volume-based hiring to a performance-led, skills-as-currency model. Compensation strategies are increasingly performance-and-skills-driven rather than role-based, with organisations investing selectively in AI/ML, Cybersecurity, Data Science, Cloud, and Digital Engineering talent while moderating overall headcount growth in favour of leaner, high-impact teams.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders who redesign hiring and performance architecture around measurable skills outcomes — rather than job titles and seniority bands — will build more resilient, capable teams and attract higher-quality lateral talent from peers whose legacy HR models are misaligned with the skills-first market.

talent.title: Zinnov 2026: GCC Salary Hikes Stabilise; AI and ML Roles Command 30–60% Pay Premium
talent.body: Zinnov's India GCC View 2026 report on salary, attrition, and hiring trends shows overall salary increase budgets stabilising as organisations shift toward performance-driven compensation. However, niche AI/ML, Cybersecurity, Data Science, and Cloud roles continue to command 30–60% premiums, with the hardest talent gap in the 8–15 years' experience cohort across AI, cloud, and platform engineering functions.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC total compensation benchmarking must now be role-segmented — market rates for AI specialists are fundamentally decoupled from broader engineering pay bands, and applying uniform salary review cycles will drive targeted attrition among the most strategically valuable technical talent.

talent.title: Top 8 GCC Skills India 2026: GenAI Engineering, Cybersecurity, and Cloud in Peak Demand
talent.body: Savanna HR identifies the top skills in peak demand across India GCCs: GenAI engineering and LLM operations, AI/ML, cloud-native development, cybersecurity architecture, platform engineering, data analytics, product management, and actuarial and risk in BFSI. Demand for GenAI specialists has surged 300% compared to 2024, with India facing a 53% skill deficit against a projected requirement for 1 million AI professionals.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: The skill demand matrix should drive internal reskilling investment priorities — GenAI engineering and cybersecurity competencies are the highest-value and hardest to acquire externally, making build-vs-buy decisions on these capabilities among the most consequential GCC strategic choices in 2026.

talent.title: GCCs Lead India Hiring as AI Talent and Emerging Cities Outpace Traditional Hubs
talent.body: Storyboard18 reports that GCCs, AI and machine learning firms, insurance companies, and advertising firms are driving India's 2026 recruitment surge, with AI/ML hiring rising 22% year-on-year. GCCs are particularly active in emerging city talent markets where competition for AI and data talent is less intense than in Bengaluru and Hyderabad, and sector diversification into FMCG, healthcare, and industrial GCCs is broadening the overall hiring pool.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC talent acquisition leaders should expand geographic hiring strategy to cover emerging city campuses and Tier-2 engineering colleges, where AI talent is available at earlier career stages before Tier-1 market compensation norms apply and before competitor GCCs establish presence.

talent.title: Q1 2026 GCC Skills Report: AI Engineer Demand Up 300%; Mid-Level Leadership Gap Critical
talent.body: Savanna HR's Q1 2026 GCC skills demand report quantifies India's AI talent shortfall: approximately 120,000 AI professionals currently support GCC demand projected to exceed 1 million AI-related roles by end-2026. The critical shortage is in the 8–15 years' experience cohort — professionals with both technical depth and cross-functional leadership — with 70% of qualified senior GenAI engineers not actively seeking new roles, making them effectively off the open market.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: Experienced AI leads and principal engineers are inaccessible through standard recruitment channels — GCC people leaders must deploy relationship-based sourcing, accelerated internal promotion programmes, and structured leadership development to build this cohort from within rather than competing in a captured external market.

policy.title: MeitY Proposes Accelerating DPDP Enforcement to November 2026 for Significant Data Fiduciaries
policy.body: Mondaq reports that India's MeitY has proposed cutting the DPDP compliance window for Significant Data Fiduciaries from 18 months to 12 months, moving the hard enforcement deadline from May 2027 to November 2026. The accelerated timeline targets approximately 10,000 SDFs including major tech companies, GCC subsidiaries, fintech platforms, and e-commerce operators, mandating DPO appointment, DPIA completion, cross-border data transfer restrictions, and third-party audits.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC compliance leaders must immediately determine whether their India entity qualifies as an SDF and, if so, accelerate DPO hiring, data architecture investment, and consent management infrastructure to meet a November 2026 deadline that most organisations have not yet fully resourced.

policy.title: India's GCC Boom Triggers State-Level Policy Competition for AI Talent Centres
policy.body: Law.asia documents that India's GCC expansion is driving competitive state-level policy activity, with 10 states having notified, drafted, or announced dedicated GCC policies since 2024. Incentive packages now include real estate subsidies, talent development grants, SEZ access facilitation, and fast-track regulatory clearances — creating a policy environment that active GCCs can leverage for structured negotiations on expansion terms.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders planning Tier-2 city expansions or new site development should formally engage state investment boards before signing leases — the competitive pressure among states creates a structured window for incentive negotiations that can materially improve the economics of new site development in 2026.

policy.title: India DPDP Consent Manager Framework to Operationalise June–August 2026
policy.body: PwC's updated analysis of India's data protection framework notes that the Consent Manager framework, expected to be operationalised between June and August 2026, will require GCCs processing Indian consumer data to maintain interoperable consent platforms for Data Principals. The Consent Manager enables Data Principals to access, review, or withdraw consent across multiple digital services — adding new technical integration obligations for cross-border data operations.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: The imminent Consent Manager operationalisation creates a near-term technical integration requirement for any GCC processing Indian consumer data; data engineering and privacy technology teams should begin consent architecture assessments immediately to avoid rushed compliance builds in a compressed H2 2026 timeline.

tech.title: NVIDIA India: Infosys, Wipro, TCS, Persistent Deploy Enterprise AI Agents in Production
tech.body: NVIDIA's India enterprise report details how Infosys, Persistent, Tech Mahindra, and Wipro are deploying production AI agent platforms built on NVIDIA AI Enterprise across back-office, customer service, and software development workflows. Wipro's WEGA-powered AI agent solution, deployed for a major US healthcare insurance provider, is already accelerating resolution times and improving member service personalisation at operational scale — providing validated proof points for agentic AI in GCC-led delivery.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC technology leaders can now access proven AI agent use cases and implementation blueprints from India's largest SI partners, substantially reducing design risk and accelerating enterprise-grade agentic deployments within existing NVIDIA-compatible technology stacks.

tech.title: NVIDIA Computex 2026: Enterprise AI Agent Platform Launched with 17 Software Partners
tech.body: At Computex 2026, NVIDIA launched its enterprise AI Agent Toolkit with Adobe, Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, and 13 additional software partners adopting the platform. The Agent Toolkit enables enterprises to build, deploy, and manage autonomous AI agents at scale across business workflows — directly impacting GCC engineering and operations teams running these enterprise platforms for process automation and intelligent decision support.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs already running SAP, Salesforce, or ServiceNow environments gain a direct integration pathway to NVIDIA-accelerated AI agents without platform migration; GCC technology teams should begin proof-of-value assessments through existing vendor relationships before the platform matures into a competitive differentiator that peers have already deployed.

tech.title: From GenAI to Agentic AI: How Enterprise AI Fundamentally Shifted in 2026
tech.body: Apptad's enterprise AI analysis documents the 2026 transition: GenAI has evolved from content generation tools to agentic systems that plan, reason, and execute multi-step enterprise workflows with minimal human intervention. India's agentic AI market is projected to reach USD 0.59 billion in 2026, with the broader Asia-Pacific region reaching USD 2.4 billion — validating India's position as the primary agentic AI adoption and deployment market globally.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC technology investment planning must now account for the agentic AI stack — including orchestration, tool-use, memory management, and guardrails — as a distinct engineering discipline from GenAI, requiring updated skill acquisition and dedicated infrastructure budgeting separate from existing AI programmes.

tech.title: EY AIdea Survey: 91% Prioritise AI Deployment Speed; 64% Flag Data Governance as Critical Risk
tech.body: EY's AIdea of India 2026 survey of enterprise technology leaders finds that 91% cite deployment speed as the primary buy-versus-build AI factor, with organisations adopting a five-dimensional ROI model covering time saved, efficiency gains, business upside, strategic differentiation, and resilience. However, 64.5% identify data governance and security as very severe challenges, and 78% struggle with system integration — confirming these as the two binding constraints on GCC AI scale-up.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: Data governance infrastructure — not model quality or compute capacity — is the primary bottleneck on enterprise AI scaling inside GCCs; leaders who invest in data cataloguing, lineage, and access controls now will unlock materially faster AI deployment cycles across all subsequent use cases.

tech.title: GenAI and Agentic AI Redefine Enterprise DNA as Autonomous Systems Enter GCC Workflows
tech.body: CIO magazine's enterprise AI analysis documents that agentic AI systems are being deployed not as productivity tools but as structural redesigns of enterprise delivery — with India GCCs at the implementation forefront. Hybrid human-AI teams are now standard in leading GCCs handling multi-step processes in finance, compliance, engineering, and customer operations, with autonomous agents managing routine subtasks while humans own exception resolution and strategic judgement.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should treat agentic AI deployment as an organisational design challenge — not a technology procurement decision — as the highest-value outcomes require fundamentally restructuring team compositions and workflow accountabilities around human-agent collaboration rather than simply deploying automation tools.
